[164:49]to this 60-day period where they're going to work out the nuclear program.

[167:01]There is no agreement on any one of these issues.

[167:06]Like we talked about before, ever since we killed Iran's supreme leader and we waged

[167:12]this regime change war against Iran, what they have done is close the Strait of Hormuz.

[167:20]What is the point of closing the Strait?

[167:23]It is to deter any second or third or fourth attempt at regime change against their country.

[167:31]To put it very simply, that is what has happened.

[167:34]The US crossed this red line of actually attempting regime change.

[190:22]I want to get a little bit into the Supreme Court and then we'll talk about our superchats.

[190:27]The other big story from last week is on these two big Supreme Court rulings on immigration.

[190:33]And I have to say, this is some good news.

[190:37]I've been very down on the administration on almost everything, but this is one of the

[190:42]actually rare bright spots, something they're actually doing well.

[190:46]In fairness, some of their Supreme Court cases and executive orders and government actions

[190:53]that have been challenged by the federal judges are totally hopeless.

[190:58]For example, I think that overturning birthright citizenship through an executive order, obviously

[191:03]that's hopeless.

[191:05]That's never gonna happen.

[191:06]But, you know what, you throw enough at the wall and some of it is gonna stick.

[191:12]You take a maximalist approach with some of these executive orders and bureaucratic measures

[191:17]in the executive branch, it may be challenged by a federal judge if it's considered by the

[191:22]Supreme Court, it might go the direction that we want it to.

[191:26]And here's two really good examples of this.

[191:29]There were two major Supreme Court decisions on immigration last week, one concerning temporary

Topic 11: Terminating TPS & why it's justified

[191:34]protected status and another concerning asylum seekers at the southern border.

[191:40]The first is about the temporary protected status program or TPS.

[191:48]And it says that if a country is experiencing a hardship,

[191:53]like a genocide or a famine or a natural disaster,

[191:58]then refugees from those countries can come here.

[192:01]And for 18 months, they can live here

[192:04]without any legal residency.

[192:06]They're temporarily protected from deportation

[192:11]because of this unique situation that we're in.

[192:14]In other words, if refugees flee from a hurricane, an earthquake, a genocide, a civil war, and

[192:24]they're here illegally, we're not going to pick them up and send them back to this country

[192:29]to be executed or to die of starvation.

[192:32]That's the idea.

[192:34]And so the government will extend this temporary protected status on an 18 month basis, hence

[192:40]temporary, it's only supposed to go for a finite amount of time for particular countries

[192:47]where there is acute hardship.

[192:50]And this status can be extended on a rolling basis, depending upon what is happening in

[192:56]that country of origin.

[193:01]We have TPS for Venezuela because of the collapse of their economy.

[193:06]We have TPS for Haiti because their government collapsed.

[193:10]We have TPS for Syria because of the civil war.

[193:15]Now when Trump got into office in 2025, he began terminating TPS status for all these

[202:10]But the measure which can be renewed by the federal government has become all but permanent

[202:14]for recipients from some nations where crises have spanned many years.
